myth: Antibiotics can cure the common cold or flu.
fact: Colds and influenza are caused by viruses. Antibiotics only work against bacterial infections and should not be used for viral illnesses.

myth: I should stop taking antibiotics once I feel better.
fact: Follow your healthcare professional's instructions about duration. Stopping treatment without medical advice can contribute to treatment failure and antimicrobial resistance.
Leftover antibiotics may not be the correct medicine, dose, or duration for a new illness. Never reuse them without medical advice.

myth: It is okay to share antibiotics with family members.
fact: Antibiotics should never be shared. Treatment depends on the infection, the patient, allergies, and other medical conditions.
The most appropriate antibiotic depends on the type of infection. Using unnecessarily broad-spectrum antibiotics increases resistance.

myth: If antibiotics worked before, they will work again for any similar illness.
fact: Symptoms may appear similar but can have different causes. Only a healthcare professional can determine whether antibiotics are needed.

myth: Antibiotic resistance means my body becomes resistant.
fact: Resistance develops in bacteria, not in people. Resistant bacteria become harder to kill with antibiotics.

myth: Preventing infections has nothing to do with antibiotic resistance.
fact: Hand hygiene, vaccination, safe food practices, and infection prevention reduce the need for antibiotics and help slow antimicrobial resistance.
